text.title: AI-Assisted Lessons-Learned Synthesis Core Rules
text.summary: Five hard rules for using AI to synthesize lessons-learned across multiple project retros without losing fidelity.

rule.statement: Every theme in the synthesis is backed by ≥ 2 verbatim quotes from source retros, each citing source_doc + section + line range. Paraphrased "summaries" without quotes are forbidden. The LLM may add a 1-2 sentence theme description but the EVIDENCE is verbatim.

rationale.source: Anthropic citations / grounding best practices; document QA research

rule.rationale: LLMs trained to summarize will paraphrase even when instructed not to. Verbatim citation forces the model to extract real text, not invent. The reader can verify each claim. Without citations, the synthesis is a plausible-sounding fiction.

rule.statement: A theme MUST appear in ≥ 2 source retros to qualify for the synthesis. Single-mention insights go to a "long tail" appendix but not the main themes list. This kills the LLM's tendency to generate plausible themes that no actual retro mentioned.
rule.rationale: The 2-source minimum prevents the LLM from "imagining" themes ("communication issues" — true of every project; cited nowhere specifically). Multi-source validation makes the synthesis honest about which lessons have evidence.

rule.statement: Each theme has two scores: severity (0-3, based on language intensity and project impact in cited quotes) and recurrence (count of distinct retros mentioning it). Themes ranked by severity × recurrence. Forces prioritization beyond "everything is important".
rule.rationale: Severity alone ranks emotional retros over routine ones; recurrence alone misses one-time catastrophes. The product captures both: a 3-severity, 8-recurrence theme is the top action item. A 1-severity 20-recurrence is process noise. A 3-severity 2-recurrence is a near-miss to learn from.

rule.statement: The synthesis proposes 5-10 action items, each with: a named owner role (not "team"), a deadline ≤ 1 quarter from publication, success criteria. The agent may propose owners; the human-edit pass confirms or replaces them. No anonymous "we should...".

rationale.source: OKR / action-tracking research; Lencioni - The Advantage

rule.rationale: Anonymous action items don't get done. Naming an owner role (e.g., "Engineering Lead", "Delivery PM") plus a deadline turns insight into commitment. 5-10 is the count that fits a quarter; more dilutes focus.

rule.statement: The agent output is a DRAFT. A human (PM, delivery lead) reads the full synthesis, validates 100% of citations against source docs, edits language, and signs the final version. No agent output is published without this pass.

rationale.source: human-in-the-loop ML; Anthropic agent safety principles

rule.rationale: LLM citation errors run 5-15% even with grounding. Without verification, the synthesis carries authority it hasn't earned. The human pass is also the moment when nuance ("that retro lead was burned out, not a process flaw") gets added.

example.title: Theme with multi-source verbatim evidence
example.code:
Theme: Discovery → spec handoff timing Severity: 3 (high) Recurrence: 4 retros Score: 12
Evidence quotes: [project-A-retro.md L42]: "Spec arrived 2 weeks after discovery ended; half the team had already moved on mentally." [project-C-retro.md L88]: "Discovery insights got translated into spec late and lost the customer's exact words." [project-G-retro.md L17]: "By the time spec was ready, our discovery panel was unreachable for clarification." [project-K-retro.md L210]: "Spec lag is the silent project killer for us."
Action item: Owner: Delivery Lead Deadline: 2026-Q3 Action: Cap discovery-to-spec window at 5 business days; any project requiring longer triggers a written exception. Success: 80% of new projects in Q3 meet ≤ 5-day spec turnaround.
example.note: Illustrates r1 (verbatim citations), r2 (multi-source), r3 (both scores), r4 (named owner + deadline).

example.title: Hallucinated theme without citations
example.code:
Theme: Communication issues Severity: 2 Recurrence: many
We should improve communication across projects.
Problem: violates r1 (no citations), r2 (vague — no specific recurrence count), r4 (no owner, deadline, success criterion). Repair: drop this theme. It's a placeholder, not an insight. If real communication issues exist, they'll appear as specific patterns with specific quotes.
example.note: "Communication issues" is the canonical filler theme; reject every time.
